title: The quantum harmonic oscillator in a dissipative bath of anyon pairs
authors: Nils-Henrik Meyer, Michael Thorwart, Axel Pelster
abstract: We generalize the formalism of open quantum systems to introduce anyon baths. In particular, we work out a dissipative anyon bath composed of independent pairs of one-dimensional Grundberg-Hansson harmonically bound anyons, which are characterized by one statistical parameter. Using a mapping of these anyons to a bosonic bath with rescaled oscillator frequencies, we show that the original bilinear system-bath coupling assumes a particular non-polynomial form. To determine the relaxation properties, we use the imaginary-time path integral formalism together with a generalization of Wick's theorem in the form of a smearing formula. The latter allows to approximately calculate the anyon bath spectral density, which acquires a nontrivial temperature dependence. The corresponding relaxation dynamics of the dissipative harmonic oscillator in an anyon bath is found. Well defined limits are revealed for both low and high temperatures. Anyonic features turn out to be most pronounced in the regime of intermediate temperatures.
summary: This paper explores how a quantum harmonic oscillator relaxes when placed in a bath of anyon pairs. By mapping the anyonic system to a bosonic one, the authors reveal that the interaction becomes non-linear and temperature-dependent. They find that the anyon statistics directly influence the relaxation rate and can induce non-Markovian behavior. The study identifies specific temperature regimes where anyonic features are most prominent.
main_problem: Investigating the relaxation and decoherence properties of a quantum harmonic oscillator when coupled to a non-standard environment composed of anyon pairs.
main_result: The authors show that the anyon bath induces a temperature-dependent effective spectral density and non-Markovian dynamics, with the relaxation rate being directly proportional to the anyon statistical parameter.
method: The study uses an su(1,1)-Holstein-Primakoff transformation to map anyons to bosons, combined with imaginary-time and real-time path integral formalisms and a 'smearing formula' (generalized Wick's theorem) to handle non-polynomial coupling.
model_or_system: A 1D quantum harmonic oscillator coupled to an infinite set of non-interacting, harmonically bound Grundberg-Hasson anyon pairs.
key_observables: Effective bath spectral density, relaxation rate, oscillation frequency, and the boundary between coherent and incoherent relaxation regimes.
important_parameters: The anyon statistical parameter (mu), temperature (T), damping constant (gamma), and the frequency of the oscillator (omega_0).
main_assumptions: The su(1,1) mapping is valid for mu > 1/2; the results rely on a first-order cumulant expansion approximation; the bath is assumed to be initially Ohmic.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the system-bath model; Figure 2 illustrates the temperature-dependent rescaling factor; Figure 3 compares anyonic spectral density at different temperatures; Figure 4 presents a phase diagram of coherent vs. incoherent regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system-bath model, applies a mathematical mapping to a bosonic representation, derives the effective spectral density using a smearing formula, and analyzes the resulting relaxation dynamics across different temperature regimes.
why_interesting: This paper extends the theory of open quantum systems beyond standard bosonic/fermionic baths, providing a framework for understanding how anyonic statistics influence dissipation and non-Markovianity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Scheme of the system-anyon-bath model constructed in this work.

Figure 2. Temperature-dependent factor, Eq. (25, of the effective spectral density in Eq. (33). The red dashed line indicates the limit ℏβω →∞in Eq. 31, while the blue dashed line marks the limit of ℏβω →0 in Eq. 32. The dash-dotted horizontal line stands for the common constant of both limits.
Figure 3. Comparison of the anyonic spectral density J(ω, β) for different temperatures, expressed in units of ℏω0, scaled with the anyon bath spectral density J0(ω0), as a function of ω/ω0.

Figure 4. Phase diagram of the coherent-incoherent dynamical regimes in the temperature T–damping constant γ plane for various anyonic parameters µ as indicated. The separation lines are defined by the roots of the radicand of Eqs. (43) [low temperature, dashed lines] and (44) [high temperature, solid lines]. The shaded region represents the coherent regime for µ = 0.55 case (shown in red). The exact separatrix for a general value of T cannot be evaluated analytically.
